text: I'm sharing a case of Crohn's disease, patient with fibrous stenotic Crohn's disease and multiple obstructions. He was kind of debating between ongoing medical therapy or surgery, but because of the recurrent obstructions, he decided for surgery with the knowledge that medications don't typically help that much with that.

The surgeon visually inspects the bowel, identifying and marking areas with visible strictures. The markings are placed on both sides of the strictures to assess whether strictureplasty or resection will be performed.

The surgeon observes the colon and small intestine, noting their relatively normal appearance, but identifies a tight stricture at the ileocecal valve. This stricture, which was impassable during endoscopy, is planned for resection as part of the ileocolic procedure.

The surgeon examines the colon and small intestine, noting that they appear relatively normal. However, a tight stricture is identified at the ileocecal valve, which was previously impassable by endoscopy and confirmed by MRI findings.

The surgeon identifies a Meckel's diverticulum adjacent to a Crohn's disease stricture and begins mobilizing the mesentery of the Meckel's. The iliocolic pedicle is tented, and a medial to lateral dissection is initiated, resembling the approach used in right-sided cancer resections.

The surgeon performs a medial to lateral dissection for the Crohn's disease resection, similar to right-sided cancer resections, focusing on mesenteric resection rather than high ligation of the ileocolic pedicle.

The surgeon isolates and thins out the ileocolic pedicle, separately identifying and dissecting the vein and artery. The mesenteric resection is performed to address potential disease recurrence. The surgeon confirms the plan for an ileocolic resection and an additional resection near the Meckel's diverticulum and stricture area.
The surgeon isolates and thins out the ileocolic pedicle, separating the vein and artery individually. The vein is addressed first, followed by the artery.

The surgeon is performing an ileocolic resection, taking the colon mesentery while preserving the right colic vessels. The dissection progresses towards the junction of the ascending colon and the cecum.

The surgeon is dissecting the colon mesentery while preserving the right colic vessels, approaching the junction of the ascending colon and cecum. Lateral mobilization is performed to connect with the previously completed medial dissection.

The surgeon performs lateral mobilization of the bowel, joining it with the previously completed medial dissection. The hepatic flexure is not mobilized to minimize scar tissue near the ileocolic anastomosis, a common site for recurrence.

The surgeon performs an intracorporeal anastomosis without mobilizing the hepatic flexure, focusing on finishing the terminal ileum mesentery. Cautery is used to mark the division site, followed by checking tissue perfusion.

The surgeon completes the dissection of the terminal ileum mesentery, marking the division site with cautery for precision. The stapling is performed through the suprapubic port, which is also used for specimen extraction, minimizing incision size to reduce hernia risk.
The surgeon performs stapling from the suprapubic port, which is also used for specimen extraction, ensuring that only one incision exceeds eight millimeters to minimize hernia risk.

The surgeon staples the small bowel and aligns the isoperistaltic ileocolic anastomosis, creating a colotomy and enterotomy. Using a stapler through the suprapubic port, the anastomosis is initially stapled, but the small bowel pushes away, prompting an additional staple load to extend the anastomosis by about 1.5 centimeters. The surgeon then sutures the common channel closed in a two-layer fashion, starting at the near corner and imbricating back towards the far corner.

The surgeon staples the small bowel and aligns the isoperistaltic ileocolic anastomosis. A colotomy and enterotomy are performed, followed by the insertion of a stapler through the suprapubic port to complete the anastomosis, which is made slightly smaller than usual, around three and a half to four centimeters.

The surgeon evaluates the initial stapling of the small bowel, noting the bowel's displacement. A second staple load is applied to extend the anastomosis by approximately 1 to 1.5 centimeters. The surgeon then sutures the common channel closed, starting at the near corner and proceeding in a two-layer fashion, ensuring the closure is secure by imbricating the tissue back down towards the far corner.

The surgeon performs a small bowel resection, focusing on the mesentery to the Meckel's diverticulum, which is identified as the distal margin. The mesentery is dissected back to the bowel wall, revealing a window into the true small bowel mesentery. The surgeon proceeds proximally towards the area of disease, noting the abnormal thickened appearance of the Meckel's diverticulum, which is scarred to the retroperitoneum. The decision is made to resect the Meckel's diverticulum due to its potential role in obstructions and confirmed presence of Crohn's disease.

The surgeon lines up a side-to-side antiparastaltic anastomosis of the small bowel, creating otomies and using a stapler to form a wide anastomosis. The common channel is then sewn closed using a baseball stitch, starting at the near corner and running out and around, with a transition stitch over the top.

They have recently come back to the office and they are feeling very good, no longer with the sensation of early satiety and bloating and the nausea associated with the obstructions that he had been having very frequently. He has been referred back to his gastroenterologist to get on suppressive therapy to help prevent early recurrence or any subsequent need for additional surgery in the future.
